## Contents

# Enantiopure drug

An **enantiopure drug** is a pharmaceutical available in one specific [enantiomeric](/wiki/Enantiomer "Enantiomer") form. Most [biomolecules](/wiki/Biomolecule "Biomolecule") (proteins, sugars, etc.) are present in only one of many [chiral](/wiki/Chirality_(chemistry) "Chirality (chemistry)") forms, so different enantiomers of a [chiral drug](/wiki/Chiral_drugs "Chiral drugs") molecule bind differently (or not at all) to target [receptors](/wiki/Receptor_(biochemistry) "Receptor (biochemistry)").

The use of a drug with a single enantiomer intends to make it more effective. One enantiomer of a drug may have a desired beneficial effect while the other may cause serious and undesired side effects, or sometimes even beneficial but entirely different effects.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The desired enantiomer is known as an *eutomer* while the undesired enantiomer is known as the *distomer*.[[2]](#cite_note-2) When equal amounts of both enantiomers are found in a mixture, the mixture is known as a [racemic mixture](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture"). If a mixture for a drug does not have a 1:1 ratio of its enantiomers it is a candidate for an enantiopure drug.

Advances in industrial chemical processes have made it economical for pharmaceutical manufacturers to take drugs that were originally marketed as a racemic mixture and market the individual enantiomers, either by specifically manufacturing the desired enantiomer or by [resolving](/wiki/Chiral_resolution "Chiral resolution") a [racemic mixture](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture"). On a case-by-case basis, the [U.S. Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_(United_States) "Food and Drug Administration (United States)") (FDA) has allowed single enantiomers of certain drugs to be marketed under a different name than the racemic mixture.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Also case-by-case, the [United States Patent Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_Office "United States Patent Office") has granted patents for single enantiomers of certain drugs. The regulatory review for marketing approval (safety and efficacy) and for patenting (proprietary rights) is independent, and differs country by country.

## History

In 1848, Louis Pasteur became the first scientist to discover chirality and enantiomers while he was working with [tartaric acid](/wiki/Tartaric_acid "Tartaric acid"). During the experiments, he noticed that there were two crystal structures produced but these structures looked to be non-superimposable mirror images of each other; this observation of [isomers](/wiki/Isomer "Isomer") that were non-superimposable mirror images became known as [enantiomers](/wiki/Enantiomer "Enantiomer"). A couple years later, in 1857, Pasteur then discovered enantioselectivity when he noticed that the two enantiomer structures he had previously discovered metabolized at much different speeds. This suggested that one configuration was preferred over the other in vivo. As organic chemistry knowledge became more advanced, the discovery of enantioselectivity was used in the creation of enantiopure drugs.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Enantiopure drugs from chiral drugs

Chirality can be observed when the geometric properties of an object is not superimposable with its mirror image. Two forms of a molecule are formed (both mirror images) from a chiral carbon, these two forms are called enantiomers.

The formation of an enantiopure drug results from the separation of the enantiomers of a [chiral drug](/wiki/Chiral_drugs "Chiral drugs"). This separation was prompted when it was found that each enantiomer of a molecule can have different effects when used as pharmaceuticals. This is because the body is very chiral selective, reacting to each enantiomer differently and therefore producing different pharmaceutical effects.

Before a drug of a pure enantiomer can be formed, the two enantiomers must first be separated and tested. Three main techniques are used for this separation: [capillary gas chromatography](/wiki/Capillary_gas_chromatography "Capillary gas chromatography"), high performance [liquid chromatography](/wiki/Liquid_chromatography "Liquid chromatography"), and [capillary electrophoresis](/wiki/Capillary_electrophoresis "Capillary electrophoresis"). Other technique such as chiral crystallization, enzyme-based kinetic separation, and enantioselective synthesis are also used.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Importance

The body of living organisms are composed of many enantiopure chiral substances. For example, [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") that make up the proteins in the body have the same configuration, L-absolute configuration. Because of this specificity, vital processes such as constructing proteins, rely on [stereoselectivity](/wiki/Stereoselectivity "Stereoselectivity") to ensure that out of all the potential enantiomers available, the body is utilizing the correct enantiopure compound.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

*Selectivity* is a very important part of [organic synthesis](/wiki/Organic_synthesis "Organic synthesis"). In scientific papers regarding synthesis, selectivity is often listed in data tables alongside percent yield and other reaction conditions. While selectivity is deemed important in scientific literature, it has been challenging to effectively implement selectivity in [drug development](/wiki/Drug_development "Drug development") and production. A major issue with selectivity in [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals") is that a large percentage of drug syntheses by nature are not selective reactions, racemic mixtures are formed as the products. Separating racemic mixtures into their respective enantiomers takes extra time, money, and energy.

One way to separate enantiomers is to chemically convert them into species that can be separated: diastereomers. [Diastereomers](/wiki/Diastereomers "Diastereomers"), unlike enantiomers, have entirely different physical properties—boiling points, melting points, [NMR shifts](/wiki/NMR_shift "NMR shift"), solubilities—and they can be separated by conventional means such as [chromatography](/wiki/Chromatography "Chromatography") or [recrystallization](/wiki/Recrystallization_(chemistry) "Recrystallization (chemistry)"). This is a whole extra step in the synthesis process and not desirable from a manufacturing standpoint.[[7]](#cite_note-7) As a result, a number of pharmaceuticals are synthesized and marketed as a racemic mixture of enantiomers in cases where the less-effective enantiomer is benign. However, by identifying and specifically purifying the enantiomer which effectively binds to its respective [binding site](/wiki/Binding_site "Binding site") in the body, less of the drug would be needed to achieve the desired effect.[[8]](#cite_note-8) With the improvement of chiral technology, a rich repertoire of enantioselective chromatographic methods have become available for the separation of drug enantiomers on the analytical,[[9]](#cite_note-9) preparative,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and industrial scales.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Criteria

According to the FDA, the stereoisomeric composition of a chiral drug should be known, and its effects should be well-characterized from pharmacologic, toxicologic, and clinical standpoints. In order to profile the different stereoisomers of enantiopure drugs, manufacturers are urged to develop quantitative assays for individual enantiomers in *in vivo* samples early in the development stage.

Ideally, the main pharmacologic activities of the isomers should be compared in *in vitro* systems in animals. During instances when toxic findings are present beyond the natural extensions of the pharmacologic effects of the drug, toxicologic evaluation of the individual isomers in question must be completed.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Patenting

When drugs are covered under patent protection, only the pharmaceutical company that holds the patent is allowed to manufacture, market, and eventually profit from them. The lifetime of the patent varies between countries and also between drugs; in the United States, most drug patents last about twenty years.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Once the patent has expired, the drug can be manufactured and sold by other companies - at which point, it is referred to as a [generic drug](/wiki/Generic_drug "Generic drug"). Its availability on the market as a generic drug removes the monopoly of the patent holder, thereby encouraging competition and causing a significant drop in drug prices, which ensures that life-saving and important drugs reach the general population at fair prices. However, the company holding the initial patent may get a new patent by forming a new version of the drug that is significantly changed compared to the original compound.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Patentability of different isomers has been controversial over the past ten years and there have been a number of related legal issues. In making their determinations, courts have looked at factors including: (i) Whether the racemate was known in the prior art. (ii) The difficulty in resolving the enantiomers. (iii) The [stereoselectivity](/wiki/Stereoselectivity "Stereoselectivity") of the relevant receptor. (iv) Other secondary considerations of non-obviousness such as commercial success, unexpected results, and satisfaction of long-felt needs in the art. The decisions made regarding these issues have varied and there is no clear answer to the legality of patenting stereoisomers. These issues have been resolved on a case-by-case basis.[[16]](#cite_note-16) With the number of current pharmaceuticals currently being marketed as [racemic mixtures](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture"), it is likely that patentability will continue to be debated in the near future.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Ibuprofen case

There are examples of common drugs, like [ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen"), where the use of chiral switching has caused controversy. Ibuprofen is a racemic mixture where the S-enantiomer is known to play a major role in reducing inflammation as it inhibits [COX-2](/wiki/Cyclooxygenase-2 "Cyclooxygenase-2") (cyclooxygenase 2) compared to the R-enantiomer; the fact that the S-enantiomer is stronger is what led to the chiral switching. But, when the racemic ibuprofen enters the body, a little over half of the R-enantiomers experience chiral inversion and transform into the favored S-enantiomer. This observation has led to a conclusion that the racemic and the S-enantiomer are potentially biologically equivalent. Because of this and the more recent evidence suggesting that the R-enantiomer may actually contribute to COX-2 inhibition, as well, but at a slower rate, there is still debate on whether or not the chiral switching seen in ibuprofen is really advantageous or if it is just to give patent protections to the manufacturers.

## Examples

The following table lists pharmaceuticals that have been available in both [racemic](/wiki/Racemic_mixture "Racemic mixture") and single-[enantiomer](/wiki/Enantiomer "Enantiomer") form. These single-enantiomer drug switched from the respective racemic drug are referred to as [chiral switch](/wiki/Chiral_switch "Chiral switch").

| Racemic mixture | Single-enantiomer |
| --- | --- |
| [Amlodipine](/wiki/Amlodipine "Amlodipine") (Norvasc) | [Levamlodipine](/wiki/Levamlodipine "Levamlodipine") (Conjupri) |
| [Amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") (Benzedrine) | [Dextroamphetamine](/wiki/Dextroamphetamine "Dextroamphetamine") (Dexedrine) |
| [Bupivacaine](/wiki/Bupivacaine "Bupivacaine") (Marcain) | [Levobupivacaine](/wiki/Levobupivacaine "Levobupivacaine") (Chirocaine) |
| [Cetirizine](/wiki/Cetirizine "Cetirizine") (Zyrtec / Reactine) | [Levocetirizine](/wiki/Levocetirizine "Levocetirizine") (Xyzal) |
| [Chlorphenamine](/wiki/Chlorphenamine "Chlorphenamine") ([INN](/wiki/International_nonproprietary_name "International nonproprietary name")) Chlorpheniramine ([USAN](/wiki/United_States_Adopted_Name "United States Adopted Name")) (Chlor-Trimeton) | [Dexchlorpheniramine](/wiki/Dexchlorpheniramine "Dexchlorpheniramine") (Polaramine) |
| [Citalopram](/wiki/Citalopram "Citalopram") (Celexa / Cipramil) | [Escitalopram](/wiki/Escitalopram "Escitalopram") (Lexapro / Cipralex) |
| [Fenfluramine](/wiki/Fenfluramine "Fenfluramine") (Pondimin) | [Dexfenfluramine](/wiki/Dexfenfluramine "Dexfenfluramine") (Redux) |
| [Formoterol](/wiki/Formoterol "Formoterol") (Foradil) | [Arformoterol](/wiki/Arformoterol "Arformoterol") (Brovana) |
| [Ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen") (Advil / Motrin) | [Dexibuprofen](/wiki/Dexibuprofen "Dexibuprofen") (Seractil) |
| [Ketamine](/wiki/Ketamine "Ketamine") (Ketalar) | [Esketamine](/wiki/Esketamine "Esketamine") (Ketanest S) |
| [Ketoprofen](/wiki/Ketoprofen "Ketoprofen") (Actron) | [Dexketoprofen](/wiki/Dexketoprofen "Dexketoprofen") (Keral) |
| [Methylphenidate](/wiki/Methylphenidate "Methylphenidate") (Ritalin) | [Dexmethylphenidate](/wiki/Dexmethylphenidate "Dexmethylphenidate") (Focalin) |
| [Milnacipran](/wiki/Milnacipran "Milnacipran") (Ixel / Savella) | [Levomilnacipran](/wiki/Levomilnacipran "Levomilnacipran") (Fetzima) |
| [Modafinil](/wiki/Modafinil "Modafinil") (Provigil) | [Armodafinil](/wiki/Armodafinil "Armodafinil") (Nuvigil) |
| [Ofloxacin](/wiki/Ofloxacin "Ofloxacin") (Floxin) | [Levofloxacin](/wiki/Levofloxacin "Levofloxacin") (Levaquin) |
| [Omeprazole](/wiki/Omeprazole "Omeprazole") (Prilosec) | [Esomeprazole](/wiki/Esomeprazole "Esomeprazole") (Nexium) |
| [Salbutamol](/wiki/Salbutamol "Salbutamol") (Ventolin) | [Levalbuterol](/wiki/Levalbuterol "Levalbuterol") (Xopenex) |
| [Zopiclone](/wiki/Zopiclone "Zopiclone") (Imovane / Zimovane) | [Eszopiclone](/wiki/Eszopiclone "Eszopiclone") (Lunesta) |

enriched_points.expanded_content: Enantiomers can exhibit vastly different pharmacological effects; one may be therapeutically beneficial while the other could be harmful or inactive, highlighting the importance of chirality in drug design.
Propranolol, a beta-blocker, has its S-enantiomer responsible for therapeutic effects, while the R-enantiomer may lead to adverse side effects, underscoring enantiomer selection in pharmaceuticals.
The FDA mandates that chiral drugs' enantiomers be well-characterized in terms of pharmacological and toxicological profiles, enhancing drug safety and efficacy through rigorous evaluation.
